title: ⭐ Practice: our first trip to Edinburgh

questions.explanation: "We" takes the auxiliary "have": we have arrived. "Has" is only for he/she/it, and "is/are" are forms of the verb be, not the present perfect auxiliary.

questions.prompt: Complete: "My father ___ booked a hotel near the castle."
questions.explanation: "My father" is one person (he), so the auxiliary is "has": my father has booked. "Have" goes with I/you/we/they, and "is/are" are not present perfect auxiliaries.

questions.prompt: Complete: "They have ___ their bags for the trip."
questions.explanation: After "have" you need the past participle. "Pack" is a regular verb, so its participle is "packed": they have packed. "Pack" is the base form, "packs" is the present simple, and "packing" is the -ing form.

questions.prompt: Complete: "I have ___ to the museum twice."
questions.explanation: The past participle of "be" is the irregular "been": I have been to the museum. "Was" is the simple past, "be" is the base form, and "being" is the -ing form — none of these can follow "have" here.

questions.prompt: Complete: "At the airport, we check in our ___ before the flight."
questions.explanation: "Luggage" means the bags and suitcases you travel with, and you check it in at the airport. A kitchen, homework and a blackboard have nothing to do with travelling by plane.

questions.prompt: Complete: "This is my first visit to Scotland — I have ___ been here before."
questions.explanation: It is the first visit, so the experience did not happen before: we use "never" (not at any time): I have never been here. "Ever" is for questions, and "already/yet" would mean the opposite here.
